questions.title: Part 1, Round 1: What Is Hunan-style Stir-fried Beef?
questions.prompt: Step 1: Use the style + method vocabulary you learned. Which description matches Hunan-style Stir-fried Beef?

options.text: Tender beef slow-braised in dark soy and rock sugar until glossy and sweet.
Beef cubes simmered in a numbing Sichuan peppercorn broth with chili oil.
Sliced beef quickly tossed in a hot wok with smoky dried chilies, garlic and scallion — dry, fragrant, and pungently spicy.
Sliced beef gently steamed with ginger and scallion, light and clear in flavor.

questions.explanation: Hunan style = chili-garlic-smoky, dry not saucy. Stir-fried = fast wok-toss with fragrant, slightly charred edges. Together: a dry, intensely spicy plate of wok-tossed beef with dried chilies.

questions.title: Part 1, Round 1: What Does It Look Like?
questions.prompt: Step 2: Now pick the image that matches Hunan-style Stir-fried Beef.

options.text: Glossy red-braised pork belly in dark sauce.
Mapo tofu in a shallow bowl with red chili oil.
Dry-tossed chili beef on a flat plate, visible dried chilies and scallions.
A whole steamed fish with ginger slivers on a long platter.

questions.explanation: The dish is dry, wok-tossed with chilies — not saucy like (A), not in broth like (B), not steamed like (D).

questions.title: Part 1, Round 2: What Is Cantonese Steamed Fish?
questions.prompt: Step 1: Which description matches Cantonese Steamed Fish?

options.text: Whole fish deep-fried until crispy, then drowned in a thick sweet-and-sour sauce.
A whole fresh fish gently steamed with ginger and scallion, finished with hot oil and a light soy drizzle — clean, delicate, ingredient-forward.
Fish fillet braised in a fiery Sichuan chili-bean paste with numbing peppercorn.
Salted fish cured for weeks then pan-fried with garlic, intensely briny.

questions.explanation: Cantonese style = light, fresh, ingredient-forward. Steamed = gentle moist heat preserves the fish's delicate texture, no heavy sauce. Together: a clean, pale-colored plated fish with simple aromatics on top.

questions.title: Part 1, Round 2: What Does It Look Like?
questions.prompt: Step 2: Now pick the image that matches Cantonese Steamed Fish.

options.text: Deep-fried whole fish glazed in glossy red-orange sweet-and-sour sauce.
Whole steamed fish on a long white platter, ginger and scallion slivers on top.
Fish in a deep red bowl of bubbling chili-oil broth with peppercorns.
Shredded salted fish with garlic in a small ceramic dish.

questions.explanation: The chosen dish is steamed, pale, with simple aromatics — not deep-fried with heavy sauce (A), not in a spicy broth (C), not cured-salted (D).

questions.title: Part 2: Your Friend Cannot Eat Spicy Food
questions.prompt: Your friend cannot eat spicy food. You are ordering main courses together. Which dish must they AVOID?

options.name: Cantonese Steamed Sea Bass
options.text: Cantonese Steamed Sea Bass — $28
options.detail: Cantonese = light / fresh. Steamed = gentle moist heat. No chili.

options.name: Hunan-style Stir-fried Beef
options.text: Hunan-style Stir-fried Beef — $24
options.detail: Hunan prefix = chili-garlic-smoky cuisine, almost always spicy. Contains dried and fresh chilies.

options.name: Stir-fried Seasonal Greens (qing-chao)
options.text: Stir-fried Seasonal Greens (qing-chao) — $14
options.detail: qing-chao literally 'clear stir-fry': just oil, garlic, salt. Not spicy.

options.name: Red-braised Pork Belly
options.text: Red-braised Pork Belly — $22
options.detail: Soy + rock sugar braise, sweet-savory. No chili.

questions.explanation: The Hunan prefix signals a chili-heavy regional style. Apply the style + method vocabulary and you can rule out the safe options immediately.
